archetypes.name: ✨ Golden Age Return Age of restoration
📜 All Prophecy Archetypes Browse all prophecies

keyMyths.title: The Prophecy of Zarathushtra
keyMyths.description: Prior to Zarathushtra's birth, prophecies foretold the coming of a prophet who would reform the ancient Iranian religion and usher in an era of righteousness, battling the forces of evil and establishing a monotheistic faith. This prophecy spurred persecution of his family before his birth, attempting to prevent the fulfillment of the prophecy, but ultimately failing.

keyMyths.title: The Prophecies of the Hebrew Bible Concerning the Messiah
keyMyths.description: Various books of the Hebrew Bible contain prophecies interpreted as foretelling the arrival of a divinely appointed king or deliverer (Messiah) who would restore Israel to greatness and bring about an age of peace and justice. These prophecies, found in books like Isaiah, Micah, and Daniel, are central to Jewish messianic expectations.

keyMyths.title: The Buddhist Prophecy of Maitreya
keyMyths.description: Buddhist tradition speaks of Maitreya, a future Buddha who will appear on Earth, achieve complete enlightenment, and teach the pure Dharma, leading countless beings to liberation. His arrival is prophesied to occur when the teachings of the current Buddha, Gautama, have faded and a new age of dharma is needed.

keyMyths.title: The Prophecy of Quetzalcoatl's Return
keyMyths.description: In Mesoamerican mythology, particularly among the Aztecs, there was a prophecy that Quetzalcoatl, the feathered serpent deity, would return to reclaim his throne, either personally or through a representative. This prophecy was later exploited by the Spanish conquistadors, as some believed Hernán Cortés to be Quetzalcoatl returning.

keyMyths.title: The Voluspa's Prophecy of Baldr's Return
keyMyths.description: In Norse mythology, the Voluspa, a prophetic poem, foretells the death of the god Baldr and the subsequent Ragnarok, the twilight of the gods. However, it also prophesies Baldr's eventual return after Ragnarok, signaling a new beginning and the restoration of order.

sources.name: Mircea Eliade, *The Myth of the Eternal Return*
sources.description: A foundational text exploring the concept of cyclical time and the recurrence of mythical archetypes.
sources.title: Mircea Eliade, *The Myth of the Eternal Return*

sources.name: Joseph Campbell, *The Hero with a Thousand Faces*
sources.description: Examines the monomyth, identifying common patterns in heroic narratives across cultures, including those related to savior figures.
sources.title: Joseph Campbell, *The Hero with a Thousand Faces*

sources.name: Wendy Doniger, *The Implied Spider: Politics and Theology in Myth*
sources.description: Explores the political and theological dimensions of myth, including how messianic figures are often used to justify power structures or challenge them.
sources.title: Wendy Doniger, *The Implied Spider: Politics and Theology in Myth*

sources.name: James Frazer, *The Golden Bough*
sources.description: A comparative study of mythology and religion, identifying common themes and rituals across different cultures, including those associated with dying and rising gods.
sources.title: James Frazer, *The Golden Bough*

sources.name: Gershom Scholem, *Major Trends in Jewish Mysticism*
sources.description: Offers a detailed analysis of Jewish messianic thought and its evolution throughout history.
sources.title: Gershom Scholem, *Major Trends in Jewish Mysticism*
